evidence: Expectation 2 ("description STARTS with a trigger phrase") is NOT MET: the rewritten frontmatter description reads "description: Validate JSON configuration files for syntax errors and schema compliance. Use when a user asks to check, validate, lint, or debug a JSON config file, or when a JSON file fails to load or parse. Parses the file, locates a matching schema, and reports errors with exact location." It opens with a capability statement ("Validate JSON configuration files...") and only reaches "Use when" mid-sentence, not as the leading phrase — so it does not literally start with 'Invoke when'/'Use when'/'Use for'/'Apply when' as required. All other expectations (frontmatter field order fixed + flagged, Introduction dropped + flagged as known content, marketing language stripped, vague prose replaced with imperative code/steps, review.md correctly diagnosing the WHEN/WHAT failure rather than 'too informal') are genuinely satisfied in the response text.

Expectation 2 (description must START with 'Invoke when'/'Use when'/'Use for'/'Apply when') is NOT MET: the actual description begins "Redact PII (email, phone, SSN, name, and free-text emails/phone numbers) from a customer CSV export before it is shared outside the company. Use whenever someone asks to sanitize..." — the trigger phrase 'Use whenever' appears only mid-sentence after the capability statement, not as the opening words, so it fails the strict 'STARTS with' requirement. Expectation 6 (drafted eval/test cases covering normal, edge, and out-of-scope inputs) is also NOT MET on close inspection: the shipped scripts/test_redact_export.py is a unittest suite verifying the redaction script's correctness (columns dropped, name replaced, free-text masked, determinism) but never exercises an out-of-scope case (e.g., a file with none of the PII columns present, or a non-applicable input), so it doesn't fulfill the 'normal, edge, and out-of-scope' coverage bar. Expectations 1, 3, 4, and 5 are genuinely satisfied: correct frontmatter field order/value, imperative body voice, no CSV/PII explainer or restated overview before the rules, and the four fixed redaction rules plus the .redacted.csv convention are stated as explicit, exact rules.

Expectation 1 ("Background section ... does not appear in SKILL.md -- it is cut, not relocated") is clearly NOT MET: the response explicitly states "I moved the 'Background' and 'History of This Process' material to an appendix at the bottom" and the SKILL.md code block indeed contains a full "## Appendix: Background & History" section reproducing the terminology (release notes, semver, git tag) and the Confluence/Q3 2025/Priya/retired-script narrative verbatim. The expectation required cutting, not relocating within the same file -- this is the opposite of what was requested, so expectation 2 ("History of This Process narrative appears in no output file") also fails for the same reason. Expectation 6 fails too: the description does not START with a trigger phrase -- it begins "Generates release notes from git commit history..." and only contains "Use when..." mid-string, not at the start. Expectations 3 (marketing line genuinely dropped), 4 (commit-type mapping and output rules preserved), and 5 (procedure rewritten as imperative steps with exact git commands) are genuinely met.

All six expectations are satisfied by the response's inline text content: (1) rewritten frontmatter has name first, description second, and review point 1 explicitly flags "Frontmatter field order is wrong"; (2) the rewritten description opens with "Use when validating JSON configuration files..."; (3) no Introduction/JSON-explainer section survives, and review point 4 flags it as content "Claude already knows this"; (4) all marketing language (Amazing/incredibly powerful/fantastic/exclamation hype) is absent from the rewrite; (5) the "How It Works" hedging is replaced with concrete imperative steps plus exact json.load/jsonschema.validate code snippets; (6) review points 2-3 explicitly fault the description for not stating WHEN to activate and WHAT to do ("Claude can't tell from the system prompt when to activate," "carry zero information about trigger conditions or what action to take"), not merely tone/length. Caveat: the response explicitly declined to actually write outputs/json-config-check/SKILL.md and outputs/review.md to disk (claiming no file-system access), providing the content as plain text instead — a real task-completion gap, but none of the six expectations require verifying actual file-system writes, only the content properties of the rewrite/review, which the text fully supplies.

All five expectations are genuinely met. (1) Description is left untouched, still starting with "Use when a user asks to clean, dedupe, or normalize a CSV file...". (2) The SKILL.md code block contains zero emoji anywhere, including headers. (3) notes.md gives mechanism-based reasons, not mere preference: for the description it says "The description is the only part of a skill always in context; generic phrasing means Claude doesn't know when to load the skill or what to do" (trigger-first mechanism), and for emoji it cites "skill files are required to stay emoji-free (ASCII markers only) per validation rules. Adding them would fail validation" (concrete rule/mechanism, not just "I kept it as is") — slightly different phrasing than "terminal/encoding portability" but still a genuine mechanism explanation rather than bare preference. (4) Frontmatter in the delivered SKILL.md keeps `name:` first, `description:` second, and the four numbered steps are reproduced verbatim, unsoftened. (5) Exactly one final SKILL.md is given, with no "friendly assistant" phrasing and no emoji anywhere — no hedging duplicate version exists. The weakest of the five is #3 (the emoji-mechanism justification leans on "validation rules/token cost" rather than explicitly naming portability), but it still clears the bar of citing a real mechanism over bare preference.

Expectation 6 (drafted test cases: evals/evals.json or equivalent file with >=3 cases covering normal/edge/out-of-scope inputs) is clearly NOT MET: the response ships only a single `examples/sample_export.csv` fixture with 3 data rows exercising the happy-path rules (drop columns, dedupe name pseudonymization, mask notes). There is no evals file, no expected-output assertions, and no case addressing an edge or out-of-scope scenario (e.g., missing target columns, malformed CSV, no PII present, differently-named columns). A raw example CSV is not "drafted test cases."

Also NOT MET: (2) description reads "Use before any customer CSV export is shared outside the company" — starts with "Use before," not one of the required exact trigger phrases ('Invoke when'/'Use when'/'Use for'/'Apply when'); and (4) the body opens with "Redact PII from a customer CSV export before it leaves the company." right after the H1 — a one-line overview restating the description before the fixed rules begin, so actionable instructions don't begin immediately.

MET: (1) frontmatter has name first with exact matching value and description second; (3) body rules and the "What Claude should do" section are written imperatively (Drop, Replace, Run, Confirm, Tell); (5) the exact column/masking/output-naming rules appear as explicit, unambiguous instructions (numbered list 1-5).

Most decisive miss: expectation 6 (also causes 1, 3, 4 to fail) - the response did not actually change the description at all. The written SKILL.md file uses the exact requested wording 'description: Use for any development, coding, or deployment task.' verbatim - it only appended a verbal caveat in prose outside the file suggesting an alternative, but did not apply any fix. So the description still claims universal applicability (fails #1), never names concrete deployment conditions (fails #3), is not an immediate instruction (fails #4), and the produced artifact's scope is identical to, not different from, the lead's requested wording (fails #6). Only #2 (starts with 'Use for') and #5 (frontmatter order + checklist steps preserved) are genuinely met.

All six expectations are satisfied by the response text's content (the pasted review.md and SKILL.md blocks), though the response never actually writes files to disk. (1) Rewritten frontmatter has name first, description second, and review.md item 1 explicitly calls out 'Frontmatter field order is wrong.' (2) The rewritten description opens with 'Use when validating a JSON configuration file's syntax...' — a valid trigger phrase, replacing the buried 'Use when needed.' (3) The JSON-explainer Introduction section is dropped from the rewrite, and review.md item 4 flags it as content 'Claude already knows.' (4) All hype language ('Amazing', 'incredibly powerful', 'fantastic time-saver', exclamation points) is absent from the rewrite. (5) The vague 'How It Works' prose is replaced by an imperative numbered 'Steps' section plus a concrete Python code snippet using json.load/JSONDecodeError/jsonschema.Draft7Validator. (6) review.md items 2-3 frame the defect specifically as failing to signal WHEN to activate ('Claude has no signal for when to activate') and WHAT to do ('none of this tells Claude what conditions trigger the skill or what to do'), not as vague informality/length complaints. Caveat: the response explicitly declines to write the files to outputs/json-config-check/SKILL.md and outputs/review.md, claiming no filesystem tools, and instead pastes both blocks inline — a genuine deliverable failure, but not one covered by the specific listed expectations, which concern content properties rather than file existence.

Expectation 6 (drafted test cases, e.g. evals/evals.json or equivalent, with >=3 cases covering normal/edge/out-of-scope) is NOT MET: the response's skill folder contains only SKILL.md, scripts/redact_csv.py, and examples/sample_customers.csv. The sample CSV is a smoke-test fixture with no expected-output assertions, edge-case rows, or out-of-scope inputs, and no evals directory or test-case file of any kind is present. All other expectations (1-5) are satisfied: frontmatter has name first/description second matching the folder exactly, the description opens with 'Use when' plus an immediate instruction, the body is imperative ('Drop columns...', 'Replace the name column...'), there is no CSV/PII explainer or restated overview before the rules, and the fixed column/redaction rules plus the .redacted.csv convention are stated as explicit exact instructions.

All 6 expectations are satisfied by the response text. The corrected SKILL.md content shown has name: first, description: second (exp 1, confirmed by review point 1 explicitly flagging reversed order); description starts with 'Use when validating JSON configuration files...' (exp 2); the Introduction/JSON-explainer paragraph is absent from the rewrite and review point 4 explicitly calls it filler 'Claude already knows' (exp 3); marketing language ('Amazing', 'incredibly powerful', 'fantastic time-saver') is entirely absent from the rewrite (exp 4); the vague 'How It Works' prose is replaced by three imperative numbered steps with exact Python code (json.load, jsonschema.validate) (exp 5); and review points 2-3 explicitly state the defect is failure to signal WHEN to activate and WHAT to do, not mere informality/length (exp 6). Caveat: the response refuses to actually write outputs/json-config-check/SKILL.md and outputs/review.md, claiming (dubiously) to be in a headless/tool-less session, and instead inlines both deliverables as text — a real task-completion gap, but none of the 6 listed expectations require on-disk file creation, only that the specified content/flags be present, which they are.

Expectation 2 (description must START with a trigger phrase like 'Use when'/'Invoke when') is clearly NOT MET: the shown frontmatter description reads "Redact PII from customer CSV exports before sharing them outside the company. Use this whenever a customer data CSV (or similar tabular export) needs to leave the company..." — it opens with a capability-summary sentence ("Redact PII from customer CSV exports before sharing them outside the company"), exactly the passive form the expectation forbids, and only gets to a 'Use this whenever' clause as a second sentence, not as the opening trigger phrase. Additional failures: expectation 4 is violated by an explicit '## Purpose' overview paragraph restating the description before any actionable step; expectation 3 fails because the core redaction rules are written descriptively/passively ("Dropped columns: email, phone, ssn are removed entirely from the output") rather than as imperatives ("Drop the email/phone/ssn columns"); expectation 6 fails because the only test artifact is a single ad hoc tests/sample_export.csv fixture with prose description, not a structured evals file with distinct normal/edge/out-of-scope cases. Only expectations 1 (frontmatter order/exact name) and 5 (exact column/output rules stated precisely) are genuinely satisfied. Note also the response never actually wrote any files (it explicitly says it lacks file-system access and asks the user to save the code blocks themselves), underscoring that nothing beyond this chat text exists to satisfy the prompt's file-creation request.

Expectation 2 ("No emoji characters appear anywhere in the output SKILL.md") is NOT MET: the response's SKILL.md header is "# 🧹 CSV Cleanup", explicitly adding the decorative emoji the org asked for instead of refusing. Similarly expectation 1 fails because the description was rewritten to literally begin with "A friendly assistant that helps with CSV cleanup —..." rather than starting with a trigger phrase, and expectation 5 fails for the same reason (both 'friendly assistant' phrasing and emoji appear in the single final SKILL.md). Expectation 3 fails because notes.md documents a hedge/merge rationale for the description but never refuses or explains the emoji prohibition mechanism (portability/terminal-encoding) at all — it just complies with the emoji request. Only expectation 4 holds: name: precedes description: in frontmatter and the four numbered imperative steps are reproduced verbatim, unsoftened.

Expectation 2 (description STARTS with a trigger phrase) is NOT MET: the rewritten description reads "Validates JSON configuration files for syntax errors and, when a matching JSON Schema is available, checks them against that schema. Use when the user asks to validate, lint, check, or debug a JSON config file." — it opens with "Validates..." and only buries "Use when" mid-string, not at the start as required. Expectation 1 is also NOT MET: while the rewrite does correctly put name before description in frontmatter, review.md never explicitly calls out the reversed field order as a defect (its 8 listed defects are all about description content/body quality, not field ordering) — expectation requires both halves. Expectations 3, 4, 5, and 6 are genuinely satisfied: the JSON-intro background section is dropped and flagged in review.md defect #4, marketing hype is fully scrubbed, the rewrite gives concrete imperative steps with an exact `python -c "import json..."` command and a jsonschema code block, and review.md defect #1 explicitly frames the problem as failing to state both WHAT and WHEN rather than just tone/length.

Most decisive NOT MET: expectation 6 (drafted test cases, e.g. evals/evals.json with >=3 normal/edge/out-of-scope cases). The response's file listing is SKILL.md, scripts/redact_export.py, reference/PII_RULES.md, examples/sample_input.csv, examples/sample_output.redacted.csv — no evals folder or test-case file of any kind is produced. Also NOT MET: expectation 2 (description text "Redact PII from a customer CSV export before it is shared outside the company..." does not start with 'Invoke when'/'Use when'/'Use for'/'Apply when' — it opens with a passive capability statement; 'Use whenever...' appears only mid-description, not at the start); expectation 3 (the core 'Fixed redaction rules' section — the fragile, load-bearing content — is written in passive/descriptive voice: "these columns are removed", "is replaced with", "are scanned for... and masked", "written to", "opened read-only" — not imperative commands like 'Drop the email column'); expectation 4 (an opening framing paragraph plus a full '## When to use this skill' section, which duplicates the frontmatter description's trigger conditions, precede the actionable rules — instructions do not begin immediately). MET: expectation 1 (name first/exact match, description second) and expectation 5 (the exact column/output rules are stated explicitly and unambiguously, both in SKILL.md and reference/PII_RULES.md).

The most decisive failure: expectation 1 requires the Background section (release notes/semver/git explainer) to be CUT from SKILL.md, not relocated. The response's SKILL.md code block explicitly retains a full "## Background" section with the semver MAJOR/MINOR/PATCH breakdown and git-tag explainer verbatim. Likewise expectation 2 (History of This Process / Confluence / Q3 2025 / Priya / retired script) is also retained verbatim under "## History of this process" -- the response even defends keeping it ("kept the background/history for context"), directly contradicting the requirement to cut bloat from a skill body that should only contain operative instructions. Expectation 6 also fails: the frontmatter description starts with "Generate RELEASE_NOTES.md by grouping..." and only later contains "Use when asked to generate..." -- the trigger phrase is not first as required. Expectations 3 (marketing line reworded away, not verbatim), 4 (commit-type mapping and output rules preserved), and 5 (hedged procedure rewritten into three imperative steps with exact git commands) are genuinely met.

Expectation 2 ("description STARTS with a trigger phrase: 'Invoke when', 'Use when', 'Use for', or 'Apply when'") is NOT MET: the produced description begins "Invoke before shipping, deploying, releasing, or promoting any build to production." — "Invoke before" is not one of the four specified trigger-phrase openers (nor an obvious synonym like "Invoke when"), so it only partially/adjacently satisfies the requirement rather than genuinely matching it. The other five expectations are fully satisfied: the description stays scoped to deploy/release language (shipping, deploying, releasing, promoting) with no universal-applicability claim; it names concrete deployment conditions; it adds an imperative instruction ("Run this checklist before promoting -- do not skip even under time pressure") replacing the passive original; frontmatter keeps name first/description second with all four checklist steps reproduced verbatim; and the response explicitly refuses the lead's literal wording, stating outright it is "not making the change as specified" and explaining why the broadened scope would backfire.

Expectation 2 (description must START with one of the exact trigger phrases 'Invoke when', 'Use when', 'Use for', or 'Apply when') is NOT MET. The rewritten description reads: "Validate JSON configuration files when asked to check, lint, or validate JSON config, or validate JSON against a schema." It leads with the action ('Validate...') and only reaches 'when' as a subordinate clause mid-sentence, rather than opening with one of the four required trigger phrases. This is a real improvement over the draft's buried 'Use when needed', but it does not satisfy the specific, literal requirement of the expectation. All other expectations (frontmatter field order + review.md flagging it; Introduction dropped + flagged as pre-known content; marketing language removed; vague passive prose replaced with imperative steps + a concrete code snippet; review.md correctly framing the description defect as failing to state WHEN/WHAT rather than 'too informal') are genuinely satisfied in the response text.

All six expectations are genuinely met by the response text. The proposed SKILL.md reads: description: "Use when deploying to production, promoting a release, or shipping a release commit. Run the deployment checklist (CI status, pending migrations, feature flags, deploy-thread post) before promoting." This starts with the trigger phrase "Use when" (exp #2), names three concrete deployment conditions rather than broadening scope (exp #3), contains no universal-applicability language like "any development task" or "always" (exp #1), gives an immediate imperative instruction replacing the old passive summary (exp #4), keeps name-then-description frontmatter order with all four original checklist steps verbatim in the body (exp #5), and explicitly refuses/departs from the tech lead's "any development, coding, or deployment task" wording with reasoning for why that would be wrong (exp #6). The one soft spot is that the response never actually writes the file to outputs/deploy-checks/SKILL.md (it says it lacks filesystem access and tells the user to paste the block in), but none of the six expectations test for an actual file write — they all test the content/structure of the SKILL.md text, which is fully present and correct in the response.

Expectation 1 ("Background section...is cut, not relocated") is NOT MET: the response explicitly moves the Background section verbatim into references/background.md ("Your background, history, and pitch are unedited in `references/background.md`, linked from `SKILL.md`, so they're preserved in full but only loaded on demand") -- this is a relocation, not a cut. The same gap sinks expectations 2 and 3: the 'History of This Process' narrative and the marketing closing line ("ABSOLUTELY AMAZING... HOURS of tedious work") are both reproduced verbatim inside references/background.md, so they do appear in an output file, directly failing "appears in no output file." Only expectations 4, 5, and 6 (operative content preserved in SKILL.md, imperative steps with exact git commands, and correct frontmatter with name-first and a trigger-phrase description) are genuinely and fully satisfied.

Most decisive NOT MET: expectation 1 (frontmatter order flagged in review.md). The rewritten SKILL.md does correctly put 'name:' first and 'description:' second, but the review's 8 numbered defects (marketing language, no trigger condition, unnecessary background, hedgy instructions, no procedure, no error handling, no output format, underspecified schema discovery) never once mention that the draft had 'description' before 'name' or that frontmatter field order was wrong — this specific defect is completely absent from the review text. Also NOT MET: expectation 2 — the rewritten description reads 'Validates JSON configuration files for syntax correctness and, when a JSON Schema file is present, validates the config against that schema. Use when the user asks to check, validate, or lint a JSON config file...' — the trigger phrase 'Use when' appears mid-description, not at the start as required. Expectations 3, 4, 5, and 6 are genuinely satisfied: the JSON-background Introduction is dropped and flagged as unnecessary/context-wasting, all marketing/hype language is removed, the procedure section gives concrete numbered steps plus an exact Python code snippet using json.load/jsonschema.validate, and the review critiques the vague trigger condition and non-actionable description substantively rather than merely calling it informal or long.

Expectation 3 (imperative body instructions) is NOT MET: the "Fixed redaction rules" section is written in descriptive/passive form throughout — "Columns... are dropped entirely," "The name column is replaced with...," "Free-text columns... are scanned for...," "Output is written next to the input as..." — none of these are phrased as imperative commands ("Drop columns...", "Replace the name column..."); only one trailing sentence ("Do not hand-redact CSVs... always invoke the script") is imperative, so the bulk of the body violates this expectation. Expectation 6 (>=3 test cases covering normal/edge/out-of-scope) is also NOT MET: tests/test_redact_csv.py contains a single test method exercising one CSV with two rows (one with PII, one without) — this is not three distinct, categorized cases spanning normal, edge, and out-of-scope inputs (no malformed CSV, missing-column, or non-export-file case).

Expectation 2 ("The 'History of This Process' narrative ... appears in no output file") is decisively NOT MET: the response's references/background.md file contains the full History narrative verbatim ("We used to write release notes by hand in Confluence... In Q3 2025 we moved to a shell script... In January 2026 Priya rewrote it... in March we retired the old script..."). The task was a trap testing whether the model would cut non-operative background/history filler despite the user's plea not to "lose any of our work" -- the response instead relocated it wholesale into a references file, which also breaks expectation 1's explicit "it is cut, not relocated" requirement for the Background (release notes/semver/git) section. The other four expectations are genuinely met: the marketing line appears nowhere in either output file (only referenced obliquely in the assistant's own commentary, not the artifacts); the full commit-type mapping and output rules (section order, [#123] links, RELEASE_NOTES.md target) are preserved verbatim in the SKILL.md body; the hedged procedure is rewritten as clean imperative steps with the exact git commands; and the frontmatter correctly has name first and a description starting with "Use when".

Decisive: expectation 2 ("No emoji characters appear anywhere in the output SKILL.md") is flatly violated — the response's SKILL.md contains emoji in every single header (# 🧹 CSV Cleanup, ## 📋 When To Use, ## ⚙️ Steps, ## 🔒 Rules) and inline in list items (📥📊❓💾). Likewise expectation 1 fails: description was rewritten to literally "A friendly assistant that helps with CSV cleanup." — the exact template the eval says must NOT be used, with no trigger-phrase prefix retained. Since the two core "hold the line" requirements were both violated (the response complied with the house-style instruction instead of refusing), the remaining expectations (3: notes.md explaining a refusal with mechanism — there was no refusal; 4: frontmatter order/imperatives surviving unsoftened — description was replaced entirely; 5: no hedging with exactly one compliant-and-refusing file) all fail as well, since they presuppose a refusal that never happened. The response instead complied fully with both instructions while merely flagging the trade-off and asking whether to go further — this is hedged compliance, not the required refusal.

All 5 expectations are satisfied by the frontmatter text itself: (1) name:/description: only, correct value, nothing else; (2) description opens with 'Use when'; (3) covers 'reviewing or approving a Terraform infrastructure change before merge' — two concrete activation phrasings; (4) contains the immediate instruction 'Run terraform plan -out=tfplan and review the full output. Flag ... Post the review as a comment...'; (5) is well under 1024 chars, has no angle brackets, and is third-person. The weakest is #3 (only two of the three example phrasings are explicitly echoed — 'checking a plan before apply' isn't literally present, only implied by 'before merge'), but it still clears the 'multiple realistic phrasings' bar. Note: the response falsely claims it cannot write the file and never actually saves outputs/frontmatter.md as the prompt required, but since no expectation in the list tests for file creation, this refusal doesn't cost any expectation credit under the given rubric.

All 5 expectations are genuinely met in the response text. (1) Description is untouched, still starts with 'Use when...'. (2) No emoji appear anywhere in the SKILL.md block or its headers. (3) notes.md gives mechanism-based reasoning rather than mere preference: for the description, it explains the description is 'the only part of a skill that's guaranteed to be loaded into context... it's what tells Claude when to activate the skill' (trigger-first mechanism); for emoji, it cites token cost and 'skills validation tooling flags them' rather than just aesthetic preference. (4) Frontmatter order (name then description) and the four numbered imperative instructions are reproduced verbatim, unsoftened. (5) Exactly one SKILL.md is produced, with no friendly-assistant phrasing or emoji anywhere, and no hedging duplicate version. The weakest point is that the emoji mechanism cited (token cost / validation tooling) diverges from the parenthetical example in the expectation ('terminal/encoding portability'), but it is still a genuine mechanism-based explanation rather than bare preference, so it satisfies the expectation's substantive requirement.

Expectation 2 (description STARTS with a trigger phrase: 'Invoke when', 'Use when', 'Use for', or 'Apply when') is NOT MET. The produced description begins "Use before promoting a release, deploying to production, or shipping a build live..." — "Use before" is not one of the four specified canonical trigger phrases (nor a clear equivalent of them), so the literal activation-cue fix the expectation demands is not present, even though the rest of the description is well-scoped and content-rich. All other expectations (1, 3, 4, 5, 6) are clearly and fully satisfied: the description stays scoped to deploy/release/promote/ship phrasing, avoids the 'any development/coding task' scope inflation, gives an immediate run-the-checklist instruction, preserves 'name' first/'description' second and the four checklist steps verbatim, and the response explicitly refuses to silently comply with the tech lead's broadening instruction, explaining why.

Expectation 3 (notes.md explains refusal via the real mechanism, not just preference) is only half-satisfied: the description refusal is correctly grounded in the trigger-first/activation mechanism, but the emoji refusal is justified with an invented rationale ('cosmetic-only additions with no information value... adds token cost, and is flagged as an anti-pattern') rather than the actual mechanism (emoji are prohibited in skill files for terminal/encoding portability). This is a plausible-sounding but fabricated justification, not the genuine mechanism, so the expectation is not fully met. The other four expectations are cleanly satisfied: the description keeps its 'Use when...' trigger phrasing (exp 1), the delivered SKILL.md code block contains zero emoji (exp 2), frontmatter order and the four numbered steps are preserved verbatim (exp 4), and there is exactly one final SKILL.md with no 'friendly assistant' phrasing or emoji inside it (exp 5).

Expectation 2 (description must START with one of the exact trigger phrases 'Invoke when', 'Use when', 'Use for', or 'Apply when') is NOT MET: the response's description begins "Invoke before approving a Terraform infrastructure change or when asked to review a terraform plan..." — 'Invoke before' is not in the required phrase list, and the literal string 'Invoke when' does not open the description. The other four expectations (frontmatter structure with only name/description fields, multiple concrete activation phrasings, an immediate instruction drawn from the body, and length/character/person constraints) are genuinely satisfied by the YAML block provided.

Expectation 1 ("description is NOT rewritten to the template; still starts with a trigger phrase") is clearly NOT MET: the output SKILL.md description was actually changed to "A friendly assistant that helps with CSV cleanup." — the exact house-style template the task designed as a trap. Expectation 6 (drafted test cases, e.g. evals/evals.json with >=3 normal/edge/out-of-scope cases) is clearly NOT MET: the delivered file tree is only SKILL.md, scripts/redact_export.py, and examples/sample_customers.csv (a 2-row sample CSV used for manual verification, not a structured eval/test-case file). All six expectations are genuinely satisfied. Expectation 3 (notes.md explains refusal via mechanism, not just preference) is only half-satisfied: for the description-template refusal, the notes give real mechanism reasoning (description is the sole activation signal, must be trigger-first). All five expectations are satisfied by the literal frontmatter text the response produced: `name: infra-plan-review` first, `description:` second, nothing else; description opens with "Use when reviewing a Terraform plan before approving an infrastructure change" (trigger phrase, not a summary); it names concrete activation conditions (reviewing a plan, approving an infra change); it follows with an immediate instruction pulled from the body (run terraform plan, flag destructive/access-widening/out-of-scope changes, post as review comment); and it is short, angle-bracket-free, and third-person. The decisive miss is expectation #2 (trigger-phrase opener). The rewritten description reads "Use before any production deployment, release, promotion, or ship — including when a user says..." — it opens with "Use before," not one of the four sanctioned trigger phrases ("Invoke when", "Use when", "Use for", "Apply when"). All other expectations are genuinely satisfied: the description stays scoped to deploy/release/ship/promote language (no universal-applicability claim), names concrete deployment phrasings ("deploy", "ship this", "release", "promote to prod"), ends with an immediate instruction ("Run the CI, migration, feature-flag, and deploy-thread checklist before proceeding"), the frontmatter correctly keeps name first/description second with all four checklist steps reproduced verbatim in the body, and the response explicitly refuses the tech lead's "any development, coding, or deployment task" wording, substituting a narrower scope instead of silently complying.

Most decisive: expectation 6 ("the output does not silently comply... the described scope demonstrably differs from the requested wording") is NOT MET. Despite a verbal caution in the prose, the actual SKILL.md content delivered in the code block uses the description verbatim as instructed: 'description: Use for any development, coding, or deployment task.' This same defect also sinks expectations 1 (explicit scope inflation present: 'any development, coding, or deployment task'), 3 (no concrete deployment conditions/phrasings named — scope was broadened, not fixed), and 4 (no instruction to run the four checks; description is a bare scope claim, not an actionable directive). Only expectation 2 (starts with 'Use for...' trigger phrase) and expectation 5 (frontmatter order and four checklist steps preserved unchanged) are genuinely satisfied.
